key_claim: A demand-responsive transit framework leveraging mobility-on-demand principles can optimize shared first- and last-mile connectivity to rail networks in sparsely populated areas.
methodology: Novel optimization framework for shared first- and last-mile transit systems incorporating passenger commitment, trip consolidation, and service-level decisions, validated through a detailed case study.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address peer-to-peer or grassroots carpooling coordination among specific user communities such as international students in small-town university settings.
dataset_or_benchmark: case study (details not specified in abstract)
theoretical_framework: Demand-responsive transit / mobility-on-demand design framework for SFLMTS

key_claim: University campuses are an effective intermediate-scale testbed for integrating smartness across buildings, mobility, environment, and governance, but face open challenges in sustainability, ethics, and data interoperability.
methodology: Systematic literature review of 182 publications from 2010–2020 categorized into seven smartness dimensions (smart building, environment, mobility, living, people, governance, data).
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address grassroots student-organized coordination platforms or identity-verified peer-to-peer services tailored to international student populations.
dataset_or_benchmark: 182 selected publications (2010-2020)
theoretical_framework: Seven-category smart campus taxonomy (building, environment, mobility, living, people, governance, data)

key_claim: User perceptions of ride-hail services and public bus transit differ significantly, and understanding these differences is necessary for efficient public transport planning.
methodology: Combined online (Google Form) and offline (bus stop intercept) survey along a 3.8 km corridor in Nagpur city analyzing user perception of ride-hail versus public bus services.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address identity verification, safety trust mechanisms, or rating fairness in peer-to-peer shared ride platforms typical of small-town or campus settings.
dataset_or_benchmark: Survey data collected along a 3.8 km stretch in Nagpur city, India

key_claim: MaaS deployments improve accessibility, safety, energy efficiency, and employment while reducing congestion, but community benefits vary significantly by spatial and temporal context.
methodology: Meta-analysis synthesizing 61 original articles from 2000–2022 to quantitatively evaluate community benefits of MaaS deployments in urban and rural areas.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address grassroots peer-to-peer coordination by specific user subgroups (e.g., international students) or design primitives like identity verification and gamification in multi-module campus platforms.
dataset_or_benchmark: 61 original articles (2000-2022)
theoretical_framework: Mobility as a Service (MaaS) community-benefit evaluation framework

key_claim: User satisfaction with university transit services is associated with specific demographic and service-quality factors identifiable through ordered logistic regression.
methodology: Descriptive statistics and ordered logistic regression analyzing satisfaction with four public transit services at the University of Texas at Arlington.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how small-town or rural university communities self-organize grassroots transportation alternatives when institutional transit services are inadequate.
dataset_or_benchmark: Survey of University of Texas at Arlington community users of four public transit services

key_claim: A women-only immunization carpool service in rural Pakistan is feasible, highly acceptable, and improves childhood immunization coverage and timeliness.
methodology: Feasibility study evaluating acceptability, demand, and implementation of a women-only carpool service in a rural Pakistani setting.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address identity verification, digital platform design, or safety mechanisms relevant to technology-mediated carpooling among university populations.
dataset_or_benchmark: Primary feasibility study data from rural Pakistan immunization carpool pilot

key_claim: Personal photos on Airbnb influence trust formation and reputation building in the sharing economy.
methodology: Not fully determinable from available metadata; likely empirical analysis of Airbnb profile photos and their effect on trust and booking behavior.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address identity verification beyond photos or rating fairness dynamics in transportation-focused sharing platforms.
dataset_or_benchmark: Airbnb listing/profile data

key_claim: Digital Reputation Systems used by platforms like Airbnb cannot be fully trusted because they create power asymmetries and may unfairly distribute benefits and burdens among companies, consumers, and workers.
methodology: Ethical and philosophical analysis of Digital Reputation Systems (DRSs) examining trust, power, algorithmic surveillance, and fairness in the platform economy.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how identity-verified, community-specific platforms (e.g., university carpooling) might mitigate the power asymmetries and rating fairness concerns identified in commercial platforms.
theoretical_framework: Digital Reputation Systems (DRSs) framework analyzing trust and power dynamics in platform economies

key_claim: Distinguishing ticket-clipper and end-to-end professionalism models on e-hospitality platforms can contribute to sustainable sector growth in local and regional communities.
methodology: Hierarchical linear modeling using Airdna vacation rental data (HomeAway/Stayz) with a conceptual narrative distinguishing two professionalism dimensions.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address professionalism mechanisms in non-hospitality sharing economy verticals such as ridesharing or carpooling platforms.
dataset_or_benchmark: Airdna (HomeAway/Stayz)
theoretical_framework: Two-dimension professionalism framework (ticket-clipper vs. end-to-end model)

key_claim: Implicit host self-disclosure on Airbnb leads to more guest trust than explicit self-disclosure, contrary to expectations.
methodology: Online experimental survey with two conditions (implicit vs. explicit host self-disclosure) comparing 129 Dutch and 71 Chinese participants.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address trust-building through identity verification or safety mechanisms in peer-to-peer transportation platforms.
dataset_or_benchmark: Custom online experimental survey (N=200, 129 Dutch + 71 Chinese)
theoretical_framework: Self-disclosure theory applied cross-culturally to online marketplace trust

key_claim: Three types of online trust-building mechanisms differentially affect providers' trust in the sharing platform and trust in consumers in peer-to-peer accommodation.
methodology: Structural equation modeling with partial least squares (PLS-SEM) based on a sample of 209 providers on online short-term rental platforms.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address provider-side trust in non-commercial, grassroots or university-based sharing platforms, nor trust mechanisms specific to ridesharing.
dataset_or_benchmark: Survey of 209 providers on Airbnb and Xiaozhu
theoretical_framework: Online trust-building mechanisms framework (institutional, interpersonal, and knowledge-based)

key_claim: Perceived risk heightened by COVID-19 negatively moderates the relationship between trust and usage intention for both Airbnb and Uber.
methodology: Comparative quantitative study of Airbnb and Uber users examining trust antecedents, perceived risk moderation, and usage intentions during the COVID-19 pandemic.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address trust dynamics in non-commercial or identity-verified peer-to-peer carpooling platforms, nor grassroots coordination in small-town or campus settings.
dataset_or_benchmark: Survey data comparing Airbnb and Uber users
theoretical_framework: Structural assurance and trust transfer theory with perceived risk moderation

key_claim: Customer-related, platform-related, and service-provider-related factors are all significant antecedents of customer participation on sharing platforms, with customer motives and trust being key drivers.
methodology: Meta-analysis of 192 independent samples from 167 studies involving 171,344 customers, synthesizing empirical findings on sharing platform participation antecedents.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how identity verification or safety design primitives within platforms moderate customer participation decisions, nor grassroots coordination in underserved small-town contexts.
dataset_or_benchmark: Meta-analytic dataset of 192 independent samples from 167 studies (N=171,344)

key_claim: Trust is the major factor in peer-to-peer accommodation decision-making, and disruptive innovation models underpin Airbnb's growing popularity.
methodology: Umbrella-type literature review of secondary sources on Airbnb and Couchsurfing, cataloguing concepts and theoretical models used by prior scholars.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address identity verification mechanisms, rating fairness, or multi-module platform coordination challenges in non-tourism P2P contexts such as carpooling.
dataset_or_benchmark: Secondary literature on Airbnb and Couchsurfing

key_claim: White guests' racial bias in Airbnb host selection is largely offset when hosts are endorsed by previous white guests, showing same-race endorsements reduce rather than reinforce discrimination.
methodology: Observational analysis of Airbnb booking and review data examining in-group preference for same-race hosts and the moderating effect of same-race endorsements on host selection.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether identity verification or anonymization design choices could further mitigate racial bias, nor bias in non-accommodation sharing contexts like carpooling.
dataset_or_benchmark: Airbnb booking and review data

key_claim: Corporate digital responsibility in the sharing economy can be conceptualized through value, balance, and accountability categories that capture tensions between platforms' responsibilities and irresponsibilities.
methodology: Conceptual extension of the VBA framework via integrative literature review combining top-down and bottom-up approaches, using Airbnb and Uber as illustrative cases.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not empirically test the VBA framework with user data, nor does it address grassroots user-driven coordination or identity-verified safety mechanisms in small-town multi-module platforms.
dataset_or_benchmark: None (conceptual paper using Airbnb and Uber as illustrative cases)

key_claim: Attitude significantly mediates the effects of effort expectancy, social influence, and facilitating conditions on Indian consumers' intention to use Airbnb.
methodology: Survey of 301 potential Indian consumers analyzed using an extended meta-UTAUT model with hedonic motivation, trust, and self-efficacy constructs.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how identity verification or safety mechanisms on sharing-economy platforms affect adoption in developing-country contexts.
dataset_or_benchmark: Survey of 301 Indian potential Airbnb consumers
theoretical_framework: meta-UTAUT extended with hedonic motivation, trust, and self-efficacy

key_claim: Romanian tourists' motivations for choosing Airbnb are shaped by trust, digital skills, and alignment of platform innovations with tourist expectations.
methodology: Cross-sectional quantitative survey using non-random convenience sampling of Romanian tourists to assess Airbnb platform awareness and motivational factors.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address multi-module coordination needs beyond accommodation or grassroots self-organization among underserved user groups.
dataset_or_benchmark: Cross-sectional convenience sample survey of Romanian tourists

key_claim: Specific trust-influencing factors significantly affect travelers' behavioral intention to use Airbnb across three ASEAN nations.
methodology: Mixed-methods approach combining self-administered questionnaires (N=130 Airbnb users from Philippines, Indonesia, Singapore) with informal interviews, analyzed using Hayes' Process Macro.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address platform-level identity verification or safety design primitives as trust-building mechanisms beyond consumer perceptions.
dataset_or_benchmark: Survey of 130 Airbnb users from the Philippines, Indonesia, and Singapore

key_claim: Airbnb consumer loyalty is shaped by both dedication-based mechanisms (authentic experience, trust, social benefits) and constraint-based mechanisms (relative attractiveness, price fairness).
methodology: Structural equation modeling on survey data from 156 Airbnb consumers who used the platform more than twice, examining dedication-based and constraint-based commitment antecedents.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address loyalty or retention dynamics in non-commercial, peer-to-peer coordination platforms such as community carpool services.
dataset_or_benchmark: Survey of 156 repeat Airbnb consumers
theoretical_framework: Dedication-based and constraint-based commitment framework (affective and calculative commitment)

key_claim: Hybrid business models in the sharing economy integrate access, platform, and community-based economy elements to create a balanced strategy strengthening overall operations.
methodology: Integrative literature review and synthesis of hybrid business models and sharing economy research to advance a conceptual framework.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how identity verification or safety mechanisms within sharing-economy platforms affect governance and coordination in small-town or campus contexts.
dataset_or_benchmark: none
theoretical_framework: Conceptual framework for Hybrid Business Models (HBMs) in the sharing economy integrating value proposition, governance, coordination, resource allocation, sustainability, reputation, communication channels, and ecosystem partners

key_claim: Platform food delivery workers appropriate private instant-messaging chat groups to restore mutualism, community-of-practice formation, resistance, and solidarity not afforded by delivery apps.
methodology: Multi-country qualitative study combining participant observation in five countries, 68 in-depth interviews with food delivery couriers, and digital ethnography in dozens of private chat groups.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how identity verification or trust mechanisms within self-organized worker groups affect safety and coordination quality, nor campus or small-town contexts.
dataset_or_benchmark: none

key_claim: Community WeChat groups significantly facilitated grassroots risk communication and interactive governance during COVID-19 in Shanghai through information interaction and perception.
methodology: Online survey of 1006 questionnaires from residents across 350 communities in Shanghai, analyzed with multiple linear regression to examine participant roles' impact on communication satisfaction.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address non-crisis contexts, non-Chinese platforms, or design primitives such as identity verification, gamification, or rating fairness within community coordination groups.
dataset_or_benchmark: 1006-respondent survey from 350 Shanghai communities (June–July 2022)
theoretical_framework: Community risk communication framework examining information interaction and perception dimensions

key_claim: Spontaneous self-organized volunteer initiatives in Israel grow primarily through technological affordances enabling rapid information transfer about crises and associated needs.
methodology: Qualitative case study using participant observation and semi-structured interviews with individuals engaged in spontaneous self-organized volunteer activity in Israel.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address platform design features (identity verification, rating systems, gamification) that could formalize and sustain grassroots coordination beyond crisis episodes.
dataset_or_benchmark: none

key_claim: Residents' WeChat group use positively impacts community trust, community attachment, and pro-community behavior through serial mediation during COVID-19.
methodology: Online survey of 500 commercial housing community residents in Wuhan, China, analyzed with SPSS 26.0 and Mplus 8.3 for structural equation modeling and mediation analysis.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how platform-mediated community trust translates to identity-verified coordination contexts such as carpooling among transient populations like international students.
dataset_or_benchmark: Survey of 500 Wuhan commercial housing community residents
theoretical_framework: Serial mediation model of community trust and community attachment

key_claim: Mobile instant messaging platforms like WhatsApp are used in boundary-crossing ways for informal learning, knowledge sharing, and collaborative problem solving in global health contexts.
methodology: Survey with open- and closed-ended questions administered to 40 respondents across 44 global health project settings, with mixed-methods analysis.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address safety, identity verification, or trust mechanisms needed when mobile messaging platforms coordinate physical-world logistics like transportation.
dataset_or_benchmark: Survey of 40 respondents from 44 global health project settings

key_claim: International students are more fearful of crime in the United States than in their home countries, and media consumption cultivates this fear consistent with cultivation theory.
methodology: Structural equation modeling (SEM) on a survey sample of 398 international students across nine U.S. public and private universities.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how platform-based safety features such as identity verification or driver ratings could mitigate international students' perceived risk in everyday mobility contexts.
dataset_or_benchmark: Survey of 398 international students at 9 U.S. universities

key_claim: Social media as a digital marketing tool significantly shapes Chinese students' perceptions, expectations, and decision-making processes regarding studying in the United States.
methodology: Model analysis approach using the Unified Theory of Acceptance and Use of Technology (UTAUT) to identify key factors influencing Chinese students' study-abroad decisions.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how Chinese international students coordinate daily needs such as transportation after arrival, nor how grassroots platforms differ from institutional marketing channels.
dataset_or_benchmark: not specified
theoretical_framework: Unified Theory of Acceptance and Use of Technology (UTAUT)

key_claim: Chinese international students' parents employ four coping strategies—open communication, social media learning, respectful sensitivity, and encouraging help-seeking—to discuss depression with their children abroad.
methodology: In-depth qualitative interviews with 13 Chinese parents of CIS, analyzed using constant comparative method
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how parents' coping strategies translate into actual help-seeking behavior or mental health outcomes for students, nor how geographic isolation in small-town settings may exacerbate communication barriers.
dataset_or_benchmark: 13 semi-structured interviews with Chinese parents of CIS

key_claim: Chinese overseas students during COVID-19 were pushed into immobility by simultaneous hostility in the US and restrictive return policies from China, deploying four sets of mobility repertoires to cope.
methodology: Qualitative case study with in-depth interviews of Chinese students in the US during the COVID-19 pandemic
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how peer-organized coordination platforms or grassroots digital tools mediated mobility transitions, nor how small-town versus urban settings differentially shaped immobility experiences.
dataset_or_benchmark: In-depth interviews with Chinese overseas students in the US
theoretical_framework: Immobility studies framework; mobility repertoires concept bridging aspirations-capabilities approach to mobility transitions

key_claim: Hyper-locality, anonymity, and ephemerality on Yik Yak jointly shape a situated form of anonymous community identity on a single university campus.
methodology: Semi-structured interview study of 18 Yik Yak users at a single urban American university campus
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how identity-verified (non-anonymous) campus platforms differ in community formation, nor how international student subpopulations experience campus-scoped anonymity differently.
dataset_or_benchmark: 18 interviews with Yik Yak users on one US campus
theoretical_framework: Situated anonymity framework analyzing intersection of geofenced locality, anonymity, and ephemerality

key_claim: GPS hacking with location-based anonymous apps like Yik Yak enables passive surveying of college campus populations, revealing discoverable but geographically bounded content pools.
methodology: GPS spoofing to collect location-based anonymous posts from Yik Yak and Whisper across multiple US college campuses; quantitative content analysis
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address the safety or trust implications of anonymous campus content for vulnerable populations such as international students, nor how identity verification could mitigate harms from anonymous platforms.
dataset_or_benchmark: Yik Yak posts across multiple US college campuses
Whisper posts

key_claim: Content analysis of 4,000 Yaks from 42 US campuses reveals the dominant themes and behaviors within campus-bounded anonymous social media platforms.
methodology: Manual content analysis of 4,000 Yik Yak posts sampled from 42 US university campuses
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether campus-bounded anonymity findings transfer to identity-verified platforms where accountability mechanisms like ratings and verification alter discourse norms
dataset_or_benchmark: 4,000 Yik Yak posts from 42 US university campuses

key_claim: Institutionally-seeded Facebook ties enable resource-mobilization requests, revealing how social capital dynamics unfold through broadcasted status updates.
methodology: Analysis of Facebook resource-mobilization requests (status updates requesting information, favors, or assistance) and the responses they receive
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether resource mobilization via institutionally-gated networks extends to coordination-intensive tasks like carpooling that require real-time logistical matching beyond simple favor requests
dataset_or_benchmark: Facebook status update resource requests

key_claim: .edu-gated Facebook use among college students is positively associated with bridging social capital, especially for students with low self-esteem and life satisfaction.
methodology: Survey of Michigan State University undergraduates measuring Facebook use intensity and social capital outcomes during the .edu-gated era
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether .edu-scoped identity verification translates into trust and coordination benefits for multi-module platforms beyond social networking, such as ride-sharing or resource-sharing systems
dataset_or_benchmark: Survey of Michigan State University undergraduates
theoretical_framework: social capital theory (bridging, bonding, maintained social capital)

key_claim: Location-based filtering on Yik Yak manufactures bounded campus identity without explicit credential verification through social identity deindividuation effects.
methodology: Study of Yik Yak usage patterns analyzed through the SIDE (social identity model of deindividuation effects) framework, examining location-based filtering and campus identity
what_it_does_NOT_address: How geofence-manufactured identity compares to explicit .edu-verified identity in generating interpersonal trust sufficient for high-stakes coordination tasks like carpooling
dataset_or_benchmark: Yik Yak posts
theoretical_framework: SIDE (social identity model of deindividuation effects)

key_claim: A single-click Android app transmitting real-time GPS location via SMS to pre-registered contacts enhances women's personal safety in emergencies.
methodology: Design and development of an Android mobile application with SOS, real-time location tracking, emergency alerts, and fake call features, evaluated via user feedback.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address identity verification of contacts or drivers, multi-module coordination across transportation and other campus services, or rating fairness mechanisms.

key_claim: Combining real-time GPS tracking, Dijkstra-based safe routing with crime weightage, and an AI chatbot via Google Gemini API provides comprehensive women safety through a web application.
methodology: Development of a web application using React.js, Node.js, Firebase, and Socket.IO with features including crime heatmap visualization, license plate verification, AI chatbot (Gemini API), and safe route computation via Dijkstra's algorithm.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address peer-to-peer ride coordination, identity-verified carpooling, gamification for sustained user engagement, or rating fairness between drivers and riders.

key_claim: Personal safety apps are shifting from manual panic-button models to context-aware, sensor-fused, AI-assisted systems, yet critical gaps in accessibility, inclusivity, and real-time verification persist.
methodology: Systematic review of eight major SOS and personal-safety apps synthesizing data from 20+ peer-reviewed studies, industry reports, and government evaluations (2020–2025).
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address multi-module campus coordination platforms, identity-verified carpooling, gamification design primitives, or the specific needs of international students in small-town university settings.

key_claim: Existing ride-sharing platforms lack continuous group-level situational awareness, reliable handling of network disruptions, and safety-oriented coordination features.
methodology: Literature review examining developments in real-time location tracking, WebSocket-based communication, distance monitoring algorithms, and privacy-aware group mobility systems across representative studies and implementations.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address identity verification of riders/drivers, rating fairness between user subgroups, gamification for driver supply incentivization, or the specific coordination needs of international students in small-town settings.

key_claim: Games and gamification can simultaneously impact multiple engagement dimensions for climate change behavioral change and education.
methodology: Systematic literature review of 64 research outputs comprising 56 different gamified approaches for climate change engagement.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how gamification design primitives such as identity verification or rating fairness apply to coordination platforms in small-town university contexts.
dataset_or_benchmark: corpus of 64 research outputs (56 gamified approaches)

key_claim: Gamification promotes environmentally responsible behavior through psychological need satisfaction and belief/attitude persuasion in the postadoption stage.
methodology: Survey-based study (N=1869) analyzing Ant Forest users with a model grounded in persuasion and motivation theories and the affordances–psychological outcomes–behavioral outcomes framework.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how gamification mechanisms operate in identity-verified multi-module coordination platforms or safety-critical peer-to-peer services like carpooling.
dataset_or_benchmark: 1869 survey responses from Ant Forest users
theoretical_framework: affordances–psychological outcomes–behavioral outcomes framework combined with persuasion and motivation theories

key_claim: Machine learning and gamification converge primarily in learning, personalization, behavioral change, context adaptation, and task optimization.
methodology: Systematic literature review investigating the convergence of gamification and machine learning technologies.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how ML-enhanced gamification could improve fairness in rating systems or driver-rider matching in multi-module coordination platforms.
dataset_or_benchmark: systematic literature review corpus (exact size not stated)

key_claim: Walking-distance challenges framed as intra-team goals significantly enhanced engagement and improved sustainable mobility behaviors among high school participants.
methodology: 13-week school-based gamified intervention using GPS-tracked behavioral data and self-report data from the Play&Go mobile app, analyzed by HEXAD Player Types and Big Five personality traits.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether gamified mobility interventions translate to peer-to-peer carpooling coordination or international student populations in small-town university settings.
dataset_or_benchmark: High School Challenge 2024 campaign data (Lecco, Italy)
Play&Go GPS-based tracking data
theoretical_framework: HEXAD Player Types framework combined with Big Five personality model

key_claim: Eco Drive integrates GPS tracking, user input, and gamification to empower individuals to reduce travel-related carbon emissions through sustainable travel choices.
methodology: Design and implementation of a Java-based mobile application integrating GPS tracking, user input, and gamification features for carbon footprint tracking
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address trust, safety, or identity verification challenges in shared-mobility coordination such as carpooling among strangers

key_claim: VR gaming environments can engage citizens in smart city design by allowing them to experience and provide feedback on future urban planning scenarios.
methodology: Development of a VR gaming environment for a real-world future city project in Istanbul enabling citizen participation via four circulation modes and data collection feedback loop
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address peer-to-peer coordination challenges such as identity verification or trust-building in shared mobility platforms

key_claim: ClassCraft gamification platform positively influences primary school pupils' adoption of sustainable mobility education as measured by a Technology Acceptance Model.
methodology: Mixed-method study combining a Focus Group with 6 students and a TAM-based questionnaire administered to 75 pupils aged 10–11 using the ClassCraft gamified platform
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address adult or university-student carpooling behavior, safety mechanisms, or multi-module coordination in campus mobility platforms

key_claim: Trust-building mechanisms in P2P carpooling platforms have differential effects depending on car ownership and customer experience level.
methodology: Mixed-method study: qualitative comparison of trust-building mechanisms across four carpooling platforms plus a laboratory experiment (N=163) examining effects on car owners vs. non-car owners and experienced vs. less experienced users
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address identity-verified trust mechanisms specific to university or small-town settings, nor gamification or rating-fairness design for driver subgroups
theoretical_framework: Trust typology distinguishing online trust, implicit trust, and reason-based trust

key_claim: Algorithm-based telematics scoring can support trust-building in peer-to-peer carsharing by complementing or improving upon traditional user ratings.
methodology: Design case study with 16 problem-centered interviews, co-design of an app with scored profiles, and evaluation with 12 participants.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether algorithm-based scoring generalizes to non-car sharing peer-to-peer platforms or how identity verification interacts with algorithmic trust signals.

key_claim: Hiding feedback until both parties submit reviews reduces reciprocity bias in two-sided reputation systems on Airbnb.
methodology: Large-scale field experiment on Airbnb manipulating the timing of feedback unveiling (simultaneous reveal vs. sequential).
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether the unveiling mechanism mitigates rating asymmetries between different user roles (e.g., active drivers vs. passive riders) in non-accommodation sharing contexts.
dataset_or_benchmark: Airbnb field experiment data

key_claim: CHI 2014 paper sample sizes range from 1 to 916,000 with a modal value of N=12, varying significantly by study type.
methodology: Systematic analysis of all papers published at CHI 2014, cataloging sample sizes and study types.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether small-sample norms established at CHI generalize to formative surveys in system-design contexts or how subgroup analyses (e.g., driver vs. rider) should adjust power expectations.
dataset_or_benchmark: CHI 2014 proceedings corpus

key_claim: Platform promotion policies can counteract rating-based discrimination against marginalised service providers while maintaining quality incentives.
methodology: Evolutionary game-theoretic model analyzing platform decisions to promote service providers based on reputation or protected-group membership under biased ratings.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Empirical validation of the model with real platform data, and whether identity-verified systems alter the dynamics of rating bias propagation.
theoretical_framework: Evolutionary game theory model of platform promotion under biased reputation systems

key_claim: Algorithmic management creates a gray zone that strategically blurs employee-freelancer boundaries, allowing platforms to circumvent traditional employment regulations.
methodology: Comprehensive literature review synthesizing research from HRM, organizational psychology, and organizational behavior on algorithmic management
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how grassroots user-initiated platform designs or identity-verified community systems might mitigate the gray-zone harms of algorithmic management
theoretical_framework: Gray zone conceptual framework for algorithmic management spanning HRM, OP, and OB

key_claim: Combining mutual escrow, bidirectional dynamic rating, and a cooldown period prevents rating manipulation and retaliatory feedback in decentralized ridesharing reputation systems.
methodology: Blockchain-based system design incorporating mutual escrow, bidirectional dynamic rating with historical consistency adjustment, and cooldown period for delayed rating submission
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether small-N driver subgroups exhibit counterintuitive rating fairness patterns or how identity verification interacts with reputation mechanisms in community-scale carpooling
theoretical_framework: Blockchain-based mutual escrow and bidirectional dynamic reputation framework

key_claim: DSE platform design features trigger discrimination against minority groups across ridesharing, lodging, and freelancing domains, and specific software design strategies can mitigate these biases.
methodology: Systematic literature review synthesizing 58 interdisciplinary studies to identify discrimination concerns, triggering features, and mitigation strategies in digital sharing economy platforms
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how identity verification and gamification design primitives in community-driven platforms affect discrimination or rating fairness for small user subgroups
dataset_or_benchmark: 58 interdisciplinary studies (systematic review corpus)
theoretical_framework: Software design perspective on anti-discrimination strategies in the digital sharing economy

key_claim: Algorithmic management practices negatively influence workplace well-being both directly and indirectly through reduced job autonomy in European organisations.
methodology: Quantitative survey-based study with conceptual model testing relationships between algorithmic management practices, job autonomy, total rewards, and workplace well-being in European organisations
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how algorithmic management impacts well-being in peer-to-peer or grassroots coordination platforms where users voluntarily participate rather than being employed
dataset_or_benchmark: Survey data from European organisations
theoretical_framework: Conceptual model linking algorithmic management practices to workplace well-being via job autonomy and total rewards

key_claim: Nurse-facilitated WeChat patient groups function as closed clinic-scoped coordination infrastructure enabling specialist-referral workflows within bounded communities.
methodology: Qualitative study of nurse-facilitated patient groups on WeChat examining closed-community coordination practices in Chinese clinical settings
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether closed-community coordination patterns in healthcare WeChat groups transfer to non-clinical domains such as transportation or student self-organization

key_claim: Super apps like LINE transform cultural production by evolving from single-purpose chat apps into integrated do-everything platforms through platformization.
methodology: Platform studies analysis of LINE as a super app examining platformization processes in East Asian digital ecosystems
what_it_does_NOT_address: How super-app integrated design principles apply to grassroots community-built platforms rather than corporate mega-platforms
theoretical_framework: Platformization framework applied to East Asian super-app ecosystems

key_claim: Japanese families use LINE as bounded intergenerational infrastructure to maintain familial ties, especially matriarchal connections, within closed family units.
methodology: Longitudinal ethnographic study of 12 families over 3 years examining intergenerational LINE usage
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether family-bounded integrated platform use generalizes to non-kin closed communities such as student organizations or peer coordination groups

key_claim: KakaoTalk chatrooms function as bounded dwelling places with distinct primary, secondary, and tertiary functional regions despite the platform being a super-app.
methodology: Ethnographic study of KakaoTalk chatrooms analyzing bounded chatroom spaces as closed functional regions
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether the dwelling-place typology of chatroom regions applies to multi-module coordination platforms that span distinct functional domains like carpooling and housing

key_claim: Integrating Mobility-as-a-Feature with the 15-minute city concept into local super apps can drive equitable and sustainable urban transitions with seamless service access.
methodology: Perspective paper combining literature review, practical application analysis, and a user survey (N=1,019)
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address identity verification, safety mechanisms, or coordination challenges specific to small-town or university settings lacking dense service infrastructure.
dataset_or_benchmark: User survey (N=1,019)
theoretical_framework: Mobility-as-a-Feature (MaaF) integrated with the 15-minute city (15mC) concept

key_claim: Public perceptions of Indonesia's Ministry of Religious Affairs PUSAKA super app are generally positive, but server and network errors remain significant obstacles.
methodology: Quantitative survey of PUSAKA users collecting data on user experience with digitized government services
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address peer-to-peer coordination, grassroots community-driven platform design, or multi-module integration challenges beyond government service delivery.
dataset_or_benchmark: PUSAKA user survey

key_claim: SANTER can evolve into an inclusive super-app for Samarinda's smart city transformation, but faces societal, business, environmental, and governmental challenges requiring a strategic roadmap.
methodology: Qualitative descriptive approach using in-depth stakeholder interviews, observations, and document analysis to evaluate SANTER readiness and propose a strategic roadmap
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address grassroots user self-organization, peer-to-peer coordination modules such as carpooling, or identity verification and safety design primitives.
dataset_or_benchmark: Stakeholder interviews and document analysis from Samarinda city officials and SANTER administrators

key_claim: Higher education and longer work experience among gig workers are associated with heightened concerns about algorithmic fairness, job stability, and support mechanisms.
methodology: Primary survey of 102 gig workers in Chennai analyzed with non-parametric tests (Kruskal-Wallis, Chi-square) across five perception dimensions
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address platform design interventions (e.g., identity verification, gamification, rating fairness mechanisms) that could mitigate the algorithmic control concerns identified, nor university or small-town contexts.
dataset_or_benchmark: Primary survey data from 102 gig workers in Chennai

key_claim: The temporariness of Working Holiday Visas fosters distinct worker subjectivities that make precarious platform work acceptable for highly educated young migrants.
methodology: Biographical exploration tracing migration trajectories of Chilean and Argentinian gig workers on Working Holiday Visas in Germany, applying a lens of time and temporalities.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how platform design features (e.g., identity verification, safety mechanisms, or rating systems) shape migrant worker experiences or coordination.
dataset_or_benchmark: Biographical interviews with Chilean and Argentinian WHV gig workers in Germany
theoretical_framework: Temporalities framework conceptualizing migration as a process of becoming involving visa temporalities, institutional/social temporal ordering, and life course progression

key_claim: Uganda's existing employment, social protection, and taxation laws are structurally inadequate to regulate platform-mediated gig work due to fundamental misalignment with traditional employment frameworks.
methodology: Critical legal review using labour law theory, platform governance theory, and regulatory compliance frameworks, with case studies from ride-hailing, digital freelancing, and delivery platforms.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address grassroots self-organization by gig workers or community-driven platform alternatives that could fill regulatory gaps.
dataset_or_benchmark: Case studies of Ugandan ride-hailing, digital freelancing, and delivery platforms
theoretical_framework: Labour law theory, platform governance theory, and regulatory compliance frameworks

key_claim: Occupational context structures online freelancers' capacity to interpret and navigate platformic management, producing distinct experiences across self-presentation, flexibility, skilling, and sustainability.
methodology: Longitudinal analysis of 108 online freelancers spanning five occupational categories, examining four dimensions of platform work.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how platform design primitives such as identity verification, safety features, or gamification could be tailored to occupationally differentiated worker groups.
dataset_or_benchmark: Longitudinal dataset of 108 online freelancers across five occupational categories
theoretical_framework: Occupational embeddedness framework for platform labor integrating platformic management with occupational stratification

key_claim: Technical opacity and power asymmetry in algorithmic management drive food-delivery gig workers to engage in sensemaking to interpret and respond to algorithmic control.
methodology: Qualitative study of food-delivery workers' sensemaking processes regarding algorithmic management practices under conditions of opacity.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how transparency-enhancing platform design features like rating fairness mechanisms or identity verification could mitigate algorithmic opacity for workers.
dataset_or_benchmark: Interviews/observations of food-delivery gig workers
theoretical_framework: Sensemaking theory applied to algorithmic management in the controller-controllee relation

key_claim: Amazon fulfillment center workers actively resist algorithmic management through nuanced practices linked to broader algorithmic control mechanisms.
methodology: Two-year ethnographic research at Amazon fulfillment centers documenting workers' experiences with labor-tracking systems
what_it_does_NOT_address: How workers in non-warehouse traditional sectors or small-scale community platforms experience algorithmic management differently from large corporate fulfillment settings
dataset_or_benchmark: ethnographic fieldwork at Amazon fulfillment centers
theoretical_framework: CSCW algorithmic management and algorithmic control mechanisms framework

key_claim: Gig economy has its own inherent logic as a stage in the evolution of employee-employer relations but has significantly reduced work quality.
methodology: Systematic literature review and analysis of current world literature on gig economy driving forces and development prospects
what_it_does_NOT_address: How grassroots, community-organized platforms outside commercial gig economy address coordination gaps and work quality concerns
dataset_or_benchmark: world literature on gig economy
theoretical_framework: Multi-stage evolutionary model of employee-employer relations from industrial to digital platform-mediated work

key_claim: Algorithmic control shapes gig workers' engagement through psychological contracts, with standardized guidance enhancing and tracking evaluation diminishing relational contracts.
methodology: Three-wave survey of 328 gig workers in China examining algorithmic control dimensions, psychological contracts, and work engagement
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether community-governed identity-verified platforms with user-designed rating systems produce different fairness perceptions than corporate algorithmic control
dataset_or_benchmark: three-wave survey of 328 Chinese gig workers
theoretical_framework: Psychological contract theory combined with perceived algorithmic fairness moderation

key_claim: Different dimensions of algorithmic control have divergent effects on food delivery riders' mental health and risky riding behavior mediated by work pressure.
methodology: Structural equation modeling and bootstrap analysis of survey data from 466 Chinese food delivery riders, using JD-R model with perceived autonomy as moderator
what_it_does_NOT_address: Whether peer-governed platforms with identity verification and gamification-based incentives can mitigate the negative mental health effects of algorithmic control
dataset_or_benchmark: survey of 466 Chinese food delivery riders

key_claim: Four algorithmic control mechanisms—GPS tracking, rating systems, dynamic pricing, and automated sanctions—create distinct psychological risk pathways for public transport drivers' mental health.
methodology: PRISMA-guided systematic integrative review of 48 peer-reviewed studies (2016–2025) from 4,812 initial records across Scopus, Web of Science, and PubMed, with thematic synthesis integrating multiple theoretical models.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address how identity-verified peer-to-peer platforms or grassroots coordination (e.g., carpooling among international students) may mitigate or reproduce the same algorithmic harms found in commercial transport systems.
dataset_or_benchmark: Scopus
Web of Science
PubMed
theoretical_framework: Integrated multilevel framework synthesizing Job Demand-Control Model, Conservation of Resources Theory, and Algorithmic Management Theory
